---
title: "Vision-Language-Action Models"
---

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are multimodal foundation models that unify perception,
language understanding, and robot control in a single architecture. Given a camera image and
a natural language instruction, a VLA directly outputs motor commands—collapsing the
traditional perception-planning-control pipeline into one neural network. This chapter covers
VLA architectures, key models, and their role in the Physical AI Toolchain.

![Multimodal AI systems integrate vision and language understanding for robot control.
*Image: [Artificial neural network](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Artificial_neural_network.svg)
by Glosser.ca, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)*
](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Artificial_neural_network.svg/512px-Artificial_neural_network.svg.png){fig-alt="Artificial neural network diagram showing interconnected layers" width="50%"}

## What Are VLAs?

Traditional robot learning separates perception (what do I see?), planning (what should I
do?), and control (how do I move?). VLAs replace this decomposition with an end-to-end
model:

$$
a_t = \pi_\theta(I_t, l)
$$

where $I_t$ is the current image observation, $l$ is a natural language task instruction
(e.g., "pick up the red cube and place it on the blue plate"), and $a_t$ is the robot action.
The model $\pi_\theta$ is a large neural network—typically billions of parameters—trained on
both internet-scale vision-language data and robot manipulation datasets.

The key insight is that pre-training on internet data gives the model rich visual and
semantic representations (understanding objects, spatial relationships, physical affordances)
that transfer to robot control tasks, even with relatively small amounts of robot-specific
data.

## Architecture

VLA models share a common three-component architecture:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-vla-architecture
%%| fig-cap: "Vision-Language-Action Model Architecture"
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Inputs"]
        IMG[Camera Image I_t]
        LANG[Language Instruction l]
        STATE[Robot State s_t]
    end

    subgraph Encoder["Encoding"]
        VIS_ENC[Vision Encoder<br>ViT / SigLIP]
        LANG_ENC[Language Tokenizer]
        STATE_ENC[State Encoder<br>MLP]
    end

    subgraph Backbone["Foundation Model Backbone"]
        LLM[Large Language Model<br>Transformer Decoder]
    end

    subgraph Decoder["Action Decoding"]
        ACT_HEAD[Action Head<br>MLP / Diffusion / Discretization]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output"]
        ACTIONS[Robot Actions a_t<br>Δposition, Δrotation, gripper]
    end

    IMG --> VIS_ENC
    LANG --> LANG_ENC
    STATE --> STATE_ENC
    VIS_ENC -- "visual tokens" --> LLM
    LANG_ENC -- "language tokens" --> LLM
    STATE_ENC -- "state tokens" --> LLM
    LLM -- "fused representation" --> ACT_HEAD
    ACT_HEAD --> ACTIONS
```

### Vision Encoder

Converts raw camera images into a sequence of visual tokens. Most VLAs use a pre-trained
Vision Transformer (ViT), often SigLIP or DINOv2, that provides strong visual features
without task-specific training. The image is split into patches, embedded, and processed
through transformer layers to produce visual tokens.

### Language Model Backbone

A pre-trained large language model (LLM) serves as the backbone, processing interleaved
visual and language tokens. The LLM's pre-training on internet text gives it semantic
understanding of objects, actions, and spatial relationships. Models like Llama, PaLM, or
Gemma provide the backbone in current VLAs.

### Action Decoder

Maps the LLM's output representation to continuous robot actions. Three strategies exist:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Trade-offs |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Discretization** | Bin continuous actions into tokens, predict via next-token prediction | Simple, leverages LLM training; loses precision |
| **Regression MLP** | Linear/MLP head maps LLM features to continuous actions | Precise; cannot capture multi-modal distributions |
| **Diffusion head** | Denoising diffusion generates actions conditioned on LLM features | Multi-modal, precise; higher inference latency |

## Key Models

The VLA field has evolved rapidly since 2023. The following models represent the progression
from proof-of-concept to practical deployment.

### RT-2: Robotic Transformer 2

RT-2 (Brohan et al., 2023) was the first large-scale VLA, built on PaLM-E (a 12B or 55B
parameter vision-language model). It discretizes actions into text tokens and fine-tunes the
entire model on robot demonstration data. RT-2 demonstrated that internet-scale pre-training
enables semantic generalization—the robot could follow instructions involving novel object
categories it had never manipulated during robot training.

### Octo

Octo (Ghosh et al., 2024) is a generalist robot policy trained on the Open X-Embodiment
dataset spanning 25 robots and 100k+ demonstrations. It uses a transformer architecture
with a diffusion action head and supports language and goal-image conditioning. Octo is
designed as a starting point for fine-tuning: users adapt the pre-trained model to their
specific robot and task with as few as 50 demonstrations.

### OpenVLA

OpenVLA (Kim et al., 2024) is an open-source 7B parameter VLA built on Llama 2 with a
SigLIP vision encoder. It discretizes 7-DoF actions into 256 bins per dimension and predicts
action tokens autoregressively. OpenVLA achieves strong performance on the RT-2 benchmark
while being fully open-weight, enabling fine-tuning on custom hardware and datasets.

### π0 (Pi-Zero)

π0 (Black et al., 2024) introduces a flow-matching action head instead of token
discretization, enabling continuous action generation with multi-modal support. Built on a
3B parameter vision-language backbone, π0 demonstrates strong dexterous manipulation
capabilities including cloth folding, object rearrangement, and multi-step assembly tasks.

## VLA Model Comparison

| Model | Parameters | Vision Encoder | Backbone | Action Decoding | Training Data | Open Weights |
|-------|-----------|---------------|----------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **RT-2** | 12B / 55B | ViT-22B | PaLM-E | Token discretization | RT dataset (~130k demos) | No |
| **Octo** | 93M | ViT-B | Transformer | Diffusion head | Open X-Embodiment (800k demos) | Yes |
| **OpenVLA** | 7B | SigLIP | Llama 2 | Token discretization (256 bins) | Open X-Embodiment | Yes |
| **π0** | 3B | SigLIP | PaliGemma | Flow matching | Diverse multi-task (10k hrs) | Partial |

## Pre-Training and Fine-Tuning

VLA training follows a two-stage paradigm:

**Stage 1: Vision-language pre-training** on internet-scale data (image-caption pairs,
visual question answering, web text). This builds general visual and semantic representations.
This stage uses standard language modeling objectives—next-token prediction on interleaved
image-text sequences.

**Stage 2: Robot fine-tuning** on demonstration datasets. The model learns to map its
visual-semantic representations to motor commands. Fine-tuning can use:

- **Full fine-tuning**: update all parameters (requires significant GPU memory)
- **LoRA**: low-rank adaptation of attention weights (memory-efficient, faster convergence)
- **Adapter layers**: insert trainable modules between frozen backbone layers

The fine-tuning loss for a discretized VLA is the standard cross-entropy:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(I, l, a) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \sum_{d=1}^{D} \log p_\theta(a_d \mid I, l, a_{<d}) \right]
$$

where $D$ is the action dimensionality and $a_d$ is the discretized token for dimension $d$.

## Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Generalization

The most compelling property of VLAs is their ability to generalize to novel situations
without task-specific training:

**Zero-shot generalization**: the model follows instructions involving objects or concepts
it encountered during vision-language pre-training but never during robot fine-tuning. For
example, a VLA trained to pick up fruits might successfully pick up a "toy dinosaur" because
the language model understands the concept.

**Few-shot generalization**: providing a small number of demonstrations (5–50) of a new task
enables rapid adaptation, either through fine-tuning or in-context learning.

This generalization is what distinguishes VLAs from task-specific policies—a single model
can potentially handle an open-ended set of manipulation tasks specified through language.

## Language-Conditioned Control

Natural language provides an intuitive, flexible task specification:

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# VLA inference pseudocode for language-conditioned manipulation
import torch
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image

class VLAInference:
    """Pseudocode for VLA policy inference on a robot."""

    def __init__(self, model_path: str, device: str = "cuda"):
        # Load pre-trained VLA model
        # self.model = AutoModelForVLA.from_pretrained(model_path)
        # self.processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_path)
        self.device = device
        self.action_bins = 256
        self.action_ranges = {
            "x": (-0.05, 0.05),   # meters per step
            "y": (-0.05, 0.05),
            "z": (-0.05, 0.05),
            "rx": (-0.1, 0.1),    # radians per step
            "ry": (-0.1, 0.1),
            "rz": (-0.1, 0.1),
            "gripper": (0.0, 1.0),
        }

    def predict_action(
        self,
        image: np.ndarray,
        instruction: str,
        robot_state: np.ndarray,
    ) -> np.ndarray:
        """Generate robot action from image and language instruction."""
        # Preprocess inputs
        # inputs = self.processor(
        #     images=Image.fromarray(image),
        #     text=instruction,
        #     return_tensors="pt",
        # ).to(self.device)

        # Forward pass through VLA
        # action_tokens = self.model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=7)

        # Decode discretized tokens to continuous actions
        # action = self.decode_action_tokens(action_tokens)
        # return action
        pass

    def decode_action_tokens(self, tokens: torch.Tensor) -> np.ndarray:
        """Convert discretized action tokens to continuous values."""
        actions = []
        for i, (dim_name, (lo, hi)) in enumerate(self.action_ranges.items()):
            bin_idx = tokens[0, i].item()
            continuous = lo + (hi - lo) * bin_idx / (self.action_bins - 1)
            actions.append(continuous)
        return np.array(actions)

# Usage
# vla = VLAInference("path/to/model")
# action = vla.predict_action(
#     image=camera.capture(),
#     instruction="pick up the red cube and place it in the bowl",
#     robot_state=robot.get_joint_positions(),
# )
# robot.execute(action)
```

## Challenges

VLAs face several practical challenges that are active areas of research:

| Challenge | Description | Mitigation |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Inference latency** | 7B+ models require 50–200 ms per forward pass | Model distillation, quantization, speculative decoding |
| **Compute requirements** | Fine-tuning requires 4–8 GPUs with 80 GB VRAM each | LoRA, QLoRA, gradient checkpointing |
| **Safety** | Language-conditioned models can be prompted into unsafe behaviors | Action space constraints, safety filters, workspace limits |
| **Sim-to-real** | Pre-training is on real images; simulation images have a domain gap | Fine-tune on mixed real + sim data, domain randomization |
| **Action precision** | Token discretization loses fine-grained precision | Higher bin counts (512–1024), continuous heads, flow matching |
| **Temporal consistency** | Single-frame input limits understanding of dynamics | Action chunking, frame stacking, video encoders |

::: {.callout-note}
## Inference Speed and Control Frequency
Most manipulation tasks require control at 10–50 Hz. A 7B VLA running at 5 Hz is too slow
for reactive tasks. Practical deployment strategies include: running the VLA at low frequency
for high-level action chunks while a fast low-level controller interpolates at 50–200 Hz,
distilling the VLA into a smaller student model, or using INT4/INT8 quantization to reduce
inference latency to 20–30 ms on edge GPUs.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## VLA Training in the Physical AI Toolchain
The toolchain's training infrastructure supports VLA fine-tuning through AzureML compute
targets with multi-GPU configurations. VLA training jobs use the same submission workflow as
other training modalities—YAML job specifications submitted via `az ml job create` with
environment variables for model selection, dataset paths, and LoRA configuration. The GPU
nodes provisioned by the toolchain (H100 80GB, RTX PRO 6000 48GB) provide the VRAM capacity
required for VLA fine-tuning. Training metrics (loss, action accuracy, gradient norms) are
logged to MLflow, and fine-tuned model weights are registered in the AzureML model registry
for evaluation and edge deployment via the fleet deployment pipeline.
:::
